coredata.dc:title: A “on-off-on” fluorescence aptasensor using carbon quantum dots and graphene oxide for ultrasensitive detection of the major shellfish allergen Arginine kinase
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Jinru
Ai, Rong
Weng, Junjie
Li, Lingfang
Zhou, Chaosheng
Ma, Aijin
Fu, Linglin
Wang, Yanbo
coredata.dc:description: Shellfish allergy is a crucial public health problem and a severe food safety issue, resulting in an urgent need for an accurate, simple and sensitive method to detect the shellfish allergens in complex food systems. Herein, we have desighed an “on-off-on” fluorescence aptasensor based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) using prepared carboxyl groups functionalized CQDs (cCQDs) and graphene oxide (GO) for the detection and quantification of the major shellfish allergen arginine kinase (AK). The FRET aptasensor allowed for the sensitive detection of AK in the range of 0.001–10 μg/mL with the limit of detection (LOD) of 0.14 ng/mL (S/N = 3), and the limit of quantitation (LOQ) of 0.27 ng/mL (S/N = 10). This reliable, accurate and specific aptasensor with user-friendly operation may provide new perspectives in the application of fluorescence technologies for food allergens detection in the field of food safety.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Arginine kinase
Carbon quantum dots
Graphene oxide
Aptamer
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer

This work was financially supported by the Zhejiang Key Laboratory of Exploitation and Preservation of Coastal Bio-resource, Wenzhou 325005, Zhejiang, People's Republic of China (J2018002), the Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (LGC20C200002), and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31972184). All authors have read and agreed to the published version of the manuscript.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Fluorescence emission spectra of cCQDs with different dilution ratios in the presence of GO. (B) cCQDs fluorescence quenching by GO with various incubation times (n = 3). Error bars refer to standard deviation.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of the cCQD-aptamer at different concentrations of aptamer. (B) cCQDs fluorescence recovery by AK with various incubation times (n = 3). (C) Fluorescence signals of developed aptasensor of AK at different reaction temperature (n = 3). (D) Fluorescence signals of developed aptasensor of AK in PBS with different pH values (n = 3). Error bars refer to standard deviation.

(A) Fluorescence spectrum and standard curve of developed aptasensor at different concentrations of AK (n = 3). (B) Specificity of the FRET aptasensor for AK (n = 3). *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.005, and ****p < 0.0001 compared with negative control. (C) Fluorescence signals of developed aptasensor of AK at different concentrations in blank samples (n = 3). Error bars refer to standard deviation. *p < 0.05 compared with blank sample.
The scheme illustration of a “on-off-on” fluorescence aptasensor for AK detection.
Determination of AK in shellfish samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: A “on-off-on” fluorescence aptasensor using carbon quantum dots and graphene oxide for ultrasensitive detection of the major shellfish allergen Arginine kinase

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Food Safety Key Laboratory of Zhejiang Province, School of Food Science and Biotechnology, Zhejiang Gongshang University, Hangzhou 310018, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Food Safety Key Laboratory of Zhejiang Province
School of Food Science and Biotechnology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Zhejiang Mariculture Research Institute, Zhejiang Key Lab of Exploitation and Preservation of Coastal Bio-resource, Wenzhou 325005, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Zhejiang Mariculture Research Institute, Zhejiang Key Lab of Exploitation and Preservation of Coastal Bio-resource, Wenzhou 325005, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food and Health, Beijing Technology and Business University, Beijing 100048, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food and Health, Beijing Technology and Business University, Beijing 100048, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Shellfish allergy is a crucial public health problem and a severe food safety issue, resulting in an urgent need for an accurate, simple and sensitive method to detect the shellfish allergens in complex food systems. Herein, we have desighed an “on-off-on” fluorescence aptasensor based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) using prepared carboxyl groups functionalized CQDs (cCQDs) and graphene oxide (GO) for the detection and quantification of the major shellfish allergen arginine kinase (AK). The FRET aptasensor allowed for the sensitive detection of AK in the range of 0.001–10 μg/mL with the limit of detection (LOD) of 0.14 ng/mL (S/N = 3), and the limit of quantitation (LOQ) of 0.27 ng/mL (S/N = 10). This reliable, accurate and specific aptasensor with user-friendly operation may provide new perspectives in the application of fluorescence technologies for food allergens detection in the field of food safety.

ce:section.ce:para: Shellfish allergy is recognized as a critical and increasing public health problem in both children and adults [1]. Shellfish allergy prevalence estimates of 0.2–0.9% worldwide [2], 0.5% to 2.0% in USA, and to over 3.3–4% in Taiwan [3]. Shellfish such as shrimp and crab is the leading cause of IgE-mediated food hypersensitivity which may result in severe acute systemic anaphylaxis [4]. To date, the only effective prevention for allergic individuals focuses on a complete avoidance of the shellfish-containing food products. Legislation mandating labelling of allergenic ingredients contained in food products has been issued in a large number of countries across the globe [5]. More importantly, traces of food allergens may be present in processed food products due to food materials, cross-contact or cross-contamination during transportation, storage and processing. Hence, the development of reliable, feasible, specific and highly sensitive methods for tracing food allergen is extremely desirable.

Among several known allergens in shellfish, Arginine kinases (AK), a heat-stable protein with a molecular weight of 40 kDa, is considered as one of the major allergens responsible for about 51% of shellfish-caused allergic reactions [6,7]. Monitoring the presence of shellfish using AK as a biomaker is extremely important to both the food industry and allergic individuals. Current analytical methods have been developed to trace AK in foods, such as liquid chromatography tandem QTOF mass spectrometry (UPLC-QTOF-MS) [8], polymerase chain reaction (PCR) [9], immunoassay [10]. Generally, immunoassay is prone to false positives due to cross-reactivity between antibodies with non-target food components. PCR suffers from false negatives becausee of the degradation of DNA during food processing. In addition, immunoassays rely on antibodies, which are expensive and time-consuming to produce and limited in the stability and working range. Therefore, it is desirable to develop a reliable, rapid and cost-effective method for the quantification of shellfish AK.

Aptamer, single-stranded oligonucleotides or peptide sequences with a high affinity and specificity for target allergens, is a critical alternative to natural antibodies. Nucleic acid aptamers exhibit remarkable stability, for instance, they can easily renature after the denaturation process and have no differences between batches. In addition, aptamers are easily modified with different reporter molecules, and their synthesis are relatively easy [11]. Recently, several aptamer-based biosensors integrated with different reporter molecules, such as fluorescein [12], streptavidin-conjugated quantum dots (QDs) [13], and OliGreen dye [14], have been established for directly monitoring food allergens. Such methods exhibit remarkable sensitivity and selectivity based on sensitive reporter molecules and specific recognition ability of corresponding aptamers. For example, Weng et al. [13] established a microfluidic system incorporate with QDs-aptamer-GO quenching system to detect the major allergen in peanuts. The QDs-aptamer-GO complexes as probes combined with the allergen with a detection limit of 56 ng/mL, demonstrating excellent sensitivity and selectivity.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are attracting a lot of attentions due to their several advantages including small size below 10 nm, water solubility, low toxicity, high sensitivity, and size dependent luminescence emission. The CQDs can be functionalized by functional groups, such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl, and epoxy, which can result in their good water solubility, good photostability, and high surface area [15]. Owing to their inherent advantages, CQDs can be considered as fluorescent probes in optical sensors for biological analysis, such as pathogenic bacteria [16], antibiotics [17], and drugs [18]. Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) based-biosensor, an extremely distance-dependent method, has attracted many attentions in probing complex intermolecular interactions. This strategy requires spectral overlapping between the absorption of the acceptors and the emission of fluorescence donor [19]. In this mechanism, CQDs can be used as fluorescent donor and adsorb aptamers via π-π interactions to construct aptamer-CQDs probe to identify target biomolecules.

In this study, we prepared carboxyl groups functionalized CQDs (cCQDs) modified nucleic acid aptamers with higher quenching efficiency respond to GO, and employed the FRET strategy to develop a “on-off-on” fluorescence aptasensor for the rapid and highly sensitive quantification of the major shellfish allergen AK. The concept and process of the FRET aptasensor are showed in Scheme 1 . For the first time, cCQD-aptamer probe and GO were self-assembly through specific π-π interaction to effectively quench the fluorescence of the cCQD. Upon adding shellfish AK, the cCQD-aptamer was released from the GO surface and form cCQD-aptamers-AK complex, leading to significant recovery of fluorescence intensity. Thus, the fluorescence intensity of the proposed aptasensor mainly depends on the concentration of shellfish AK. Finally, we evaluated the function of our aptasensor using nine shellfish samples to determine the sensitivity, reproducibility and resolution.

Malic acid, sodium hydroxide, Tris (hydroxymethyl) aminomethane, sodium chloride, potassium chloride, mono potassium phosphate, sodium hydrogen phosphate, and hydrochloric acid were obtained from Aladdin (Los Angeles, CA, USA). Dialysis membranes (1 kDa cutoff) were obtained from Bio Basic Inc (New York, USA). Graphene oxide was purchase from XFNANO (Nanjing, China). The amidogen modified AK aptamer (5′-NH2-C6-GGCGAACAGCAGCGCGATTCGGGTTGCGGATAGTGACATA-3′) was synthesized by Sangon Biotech (Shanghai, China). Penaeus chinensis, Trachypenaeus curvirostris, Penaeus vannamei, Oratosquilla oratoria, Macrobrachium nipponense, Penaeus monodon, Shrimp meatballs, Shrimp dumpling, Shrimp mince were purchased from a local supermarket (CenturyMart, Hangzhou, China).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were acquired using a Tecnai G2 Spirit 120 kV (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Massachusetts, USA). The UV–vis spectra were measured via a UV2600 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). The fluorescence spectra were acquired using a microplate reader (Spectra Max i3x; Molecular Devices, USA). High resolution X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of cCQD was obtained on a K-Alpha+ (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Massachusetts, USA) spectrometer. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of cCQD were obtained on a Nicolet iS5 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Massachusetts, USA). The isolation and purification of proteins were achieved via an AKTApure 25 (General Electric Company, Boston, USA). All the solutions were prepared using deionized water purified from a Milli-Q system with an electric resistivity 18.2 MΩ·cm. The extract was centrifuged in a Microfuge 22 R centrifuge (Beckman Coulter, Mississauga, ON), and all the data graphs were processed by the Origin Lab 2018 software and GraphPad Prism version 7.0.

cCQD were synthesized according to Loo et al. [20]. Briefly, 6 g of malic acid was loaded into a glass beaker with a lid and then heated at 205 °C until melting. The temperature was maintained at 205 °C for 35 min and then cooled down to room temperature. 50 mL of 0.25 M NaOH was then added into the system and the PH value was then adjusted to 6.0. After that, the resulting solution was dialysed using a 1 kDa cutoff membrane for 3 days, followed by evaporation to 100 mL by vacuum evaporator at 40 °C. The cCQD were prepared, analyzed by TEM, FT-IR, XPS and UV–vis, and then stored at 4 °C.

AK was purified according to the method of Rahman et al. [21] with some modifications, and AK concentration was estimated by BCA assay. From this, the AK solution was consecutively diluted to 0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10 μg/mL as the working solutions using 10 mM PBS buffer (pH 7.4). 20 μL of cCQD was mixed with 40 μL of AK aptamers in 96 well microtiter plates, followed by stirring for 2 h at 25 °C to prepare cCQD-aptamer conjugates. After that, the cCQD-aptamer probe was mixed well with 50 μL of GO suspensions for 15 mins, resulting in the self-assembly of aptamer on the surface of GO through the π-π attraction. Then, 50 μL of each sample, includes AK solutions and sample solutions, were respectively added into the reaction system. After adjusting the total volume to 200 μL with 10 mM PBS buffer (pH 7.4), the mixtures were incubated for 1 h at 25 °C. Fluorescence intensity of mixtures were measured at 25 °C with excitation wavelength at 370 nm and the emission spectra recorded over the range of 400–500 nm.

Total shellfish protein extraction was performed using a commercial kit (KeyGEN, Nanjing, China). Briefly, 0.1 g of shrimp muscle was grounded into powder with liquid nitrogen and then mixed with the extraction buffer (1 mL of Lysis Buffer, 1 μL of protease inhibitor, 10 μL of phosphodiesterase inhibitors, 5 μL of 100 mM PMSF). After stationary incubation for 2 h at 4 °C, the extract was centrifuged at 12000×g at 4 °C for 5 mins and the supernatant was obtained. Total shellfish protein extraction was diluted 1000 times with PBS before determination. The total protein extracted was then eluted at 2 mL/min in Tris-HCl buffer (pH 8.0) using DEAE F.F. column to remove AK and the other non-allergic proteins in the shellfish samples were obtained. The concentration of total shellfish protein extraction or the other nonallergenic proteins was determined by BCA assay. The shellfish extracts and the other nonallergenic proteins was analyzed by sodium dodecyl sulfate–polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (SDS-PAGE) using AlphaView SA 3.4.0 software.

All determinations were performed at least in triplicate. Statistical significance of the data was determined by one-way analysis of variance using SPSS software (19.0, SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA). A p value of < 0.05 was considered statistically significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of cCQDs-GO FRET aptasensor for AK
ce:section.ce:para: The principle of this “ON-OFF-ON” sensor is shown in Scheme 1. Aptamer-cCQD was employed as energy donor with GO as energy acceptor to identify AK via fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process. In this study, aptamer-cCQD probe was first prepared to recognize AK, providing prominent fluorescence (Scheme 1, green curve). Aptamer-cCQD probe can be adsorbed on the surface of GO via π-π stacking interactions between GO and nucleotide base [16], resulting in FRET phenomenon and leading to quenching of fluorescence of aptamer-cCQD (Scheme 1, red curve). In the presence of the AK in samples, the association constant between the aptamer-CQDs probes and AK is bigger than that between the aptamer-CQDs probes and GO, leading to the aptamer-CQDs detached from the GO, thereby resulting in significant recovery of fluorescence of the CQDs (Scheme 1, blue curve). The fluorescence recovery was positively correlated with AK concentration. This developed aptasensor was repid and simple since cCQDs and GO were used without functionalization and FRET phenomenon was directly achieved by their intrinsic property.

Fig. S1A showed the TEM images of cCQDs with an average grain diameter of 3 nm. As fluorescence spectrum shown, the synthesized cCQDs were excited at 370 nm wavelength and the fluorescence emission intensity was observed at 420 nm (Fig. S1A). As determined from FT-IR analysis (Fig. S1B), peaks at 3240 cm−1, 1641 cm−1, and 1534 cm−1 were assigned to a secondary amine, amide CO, amide NH bending vibration [22], respectively. In addition, The XPS survey spectra of cCQDs exhibited three peaks at 300 eV, 400 eV, and 500 eV, which were assigned to C1s, O1s, and N1s (Fig. S2), indicating the cCQDs mainly consisted of O, N and C. Particularly, The XPS spectrum of cCQDs in the C1s region revealed the presence of CO, CO, CN, CC and CC. Futhermore, the UV–vis spectra of cCQDs displayed a strong absorption peak at 230 nm in the UV region with a tail extending to the visible range (Fig. S3). The maximum optical absorption at 230 nm can be assigned to the π-π* transition of CC bonds [23].

In order to develop a “ON-OFF-ON” sensor with high fluorescence turn-on ratio for the quantification of AK in shellfish sample, several crucial factors were investigated, including concertation of cCQDs, incubation time of GO, concentration of aptamer, incubation time of AK, reaction temperature, and pH of buffer.

To achieve the high quenching efficiency and good sensitivity, the fluorescence quenching of seven different dilution ratios of cCQDs (0, 10, 20, 100, 500, 1000, 2000 times) induced by GO at 25 °C was investigated (Fig. 1 A and Fig. S4). The fluorescence signal gradually decreased, and fluorescence turn-off ratio significantly increased with increasing dilution ratios of cCQDs until 1000 times. Afterwards, a slightly increased fluorescence turn-off ratio was observed. However, the fluorescence turn-on ratio and sensitivity decreased with the increase in the dilution ratios of cCQDs. Thus, the optimal dilution ratio of cCQDs was determined to be 1000 times. Subsequently, the cCQDs were incubated with GO at 25 °C for 5, 10, 15, 20, 15, 30 mins. Fig. 1B displayed that the fluorescence intensity was minimum when the incubation time of GO was 10 mins but remained stable after 15 mins. Thus, an optimum incubation time of GO at 15 mins was selected for overall detection system.

Secondly, to establish a FRET aptasensor for accurate determination of AK, four concentrations of AK-aptamer (0.5, 1, 2, 4 μmol/mL) were incubated with cCQDs as probe. Fig. 2 A revealed that the highest fluorescence intensity was observed at 470 nm when aptamer-cCQD were excited at 370 nm. Besides, the fluorescence intensity was positively correlated with the concentration of AK-aptamer then reached to the highest at 2 μmol/mL. Next, the different incubation times of AK from 30 mins to 120 mins were examined. Fig. 2B displayed an obvious increased fluorescence recovery ratio with the increase of incubation time from 30 mins to 60 mins because of higher compatibility of AK towards aptamer-cCQD probe. Afterwards, the fluorescence intensity tends to be gentle because the aptamer-cCQD probe was completely binding with AK. Thus, we selected 60 mins as the incubation time of AK.

Finally, the binding affinity of aptamers is a significant factor to affect the sensitivity and accuracy, which depends on the conformation of a given aptamer affected heavily by temperature and pH of buffer [24]. As shown in Fig. 2C, three different temperatures were compared in the reaction between aptamer-cCQD and AK. The maximum fluorescence signal was observed while 37 °C was employed. However, 25 °C was selected due to its better stability and the relatively higher fluorescence intensity. Next, the optimal pH value of PBS buffer was investigated. As shown in Fig. 2D, PBS at pH 6.0 emitted the maximum fluorescence signals but the lowest stability, while PBS at pH 7.0 showed the same fluorescence signals and the highest stability compared to other pH values.

Collectively, we decided to employ cCQDs with dilution ratio of 1000 times, incubation time of GO at 15 mins, 2 μmol/mL AK-aptamer, incubation time of AK at 60 mins, reaction temperature at 25 °C, and PBS at pH 7.0 as the optimal parameters for AK detection by a FRET aptasensor.

On the basis of the above optimal conditions, this method was evaluated in terms of calibration curve, limit of detection (LOD), limit of quantification (LOQ), specificity, precision (relative standard deviation, RSD) and accuracy (recovery).

To quantify the shellfish allergen AK, five different concentrations of AK standards (0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10 μg/mL) were measured to determine the calibration curve of this method. Fig. 3 A exhibited the fluorescence intensity was intensified by the increase of the AK concentration ranged from 0.001 to 10 μg/mL. In addition, (F-F0)/F0 was in a relationship along with concentrations of AK, which could be represented by the equation: y =
0.06077 +
0.65279
1 +
x
0.58528
- 0.5022
with good correlation (R2 = 0.9965). In this equation, F0 is the fluorescence intensity of CQDs in the absence of AK while F is the recovery fluorescence signal after the addition of AK. The LOD was estimated to be 0.14 ng/mL (S/N = 3), and the LOQ was 0.27 ng/mL (S/N = 10). Compared to a double-enhanced ELISA [25] which determined a LOD of 3.91 ng/mL, this FRET aptasensor demonstrated the higher sensitivity for the quantification of AK.

Additionally, the specificity, accuracy (recovery) and precision (intra and inter-day relative standard deviation, RSD) of this aptasensor were confirmed by analyzing the blank samples (the total protein in the shrimp except for AK) and AK standard solutions. Fig. 3B showed the presence of blank sample produced negligible fluorescent recovery of cCQDs, while AK at concentrations of 1 and 5 μg/mL has a certain degree of inevitable signal compared to the negative control. In addition, the standard recovery rate was determined by analyzing three blank samples mixed with AK standards at concentrations of 0.02, 0.05, 0.1 µg/mL, respectively. As Fig. 3C and Table S1 shown, the recovery of this developed aptasensor ranged from 92% to 110%, indicating a good accuracy for AK detection. The inter-day RSD were investigated by continuously analyzing three different concentrations (0.1, 1, 10 µg/mL) of AK within blank smples for three times, and the intra-day RSD were investigated by three parallel blank smples containing the same concentrations (0.1, 1, 10 µg/mL) of AK. These results showed that the intra-day RSD was 4.18–6.83% and the inter-day RSD was 3.17–4.89% (Table S2), suggesting that this method is quite reliable.

In order to demonstrate the ability of this developed method to function effectively in practical applications, we analyzed six different species of fresh shellfish samples and three shrimp products. According to the SDS-PAGE electropherogram of the total protein extracted from nine samples, the bands (40 kDa) of AK were observed in all the shellfish samples (Fig. S5), suggesting AK extensively existed in shellfish. All of the shllfish samples were detected by our FRET aptasensor. And then, the concentrations of AK in shellfish samples were back-calculated from the calibration curve. As shown in Table 1 , AK can be identified in these nine samples with abundances between 21.0 and 256.6 μg/g, indicating that the proposed aptasensor can be employed for practical detection of shellfish allergen AK in relevant real samples. Interestingly, the concentrations of AK in shellfish samples obtained by double-enhanced ELISA [21] were higher than we detected here, which may due to the discrepancy between two methods.

In this contribution, we have successfully developed a novel fluorescence aptasensor based on the FRET process between CQDs and GO for specific and ultrasensitive determination of shellfish allergen AK. The presented aptasensor is the most effective, cost-effective and applicable method with much lower LOD of 0.14 ng/mL (S/N = 3), and LOQ of 0.27 ng/mL (S/N = 10), demonstrating excellent sensitivity and offers higher capabilities compared to traditional biosensing approaches. Moreover, the applicability of developed aptasensor were confirmed by using nine different shellfish samples. Overall, due to the simplicity of the theory, the rapid sample procedure, and the user-friendly operation, this “on-off-on” aptasensor may offer a new direction for the ultrasensitive detection of more targets in the field of food safety.

Jinru Zhou: Conceptualization, Writing - original draft. Rong Ai: Data curation. Junjie Weng: Methodology. Lingfang Li: Resources. Chaosheng Zhou: Funding acquisition. Aijin Ma: Investigation. Linglin Fu: Project administration. Yanbo Wang: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Zhejiang Key Laboratory of Exploitation and Preservation of Coastal Bio-resource, Wenzhou 325005, Zhejiang, People’s Republic of China (J2018002), the Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (LGC20C200002), and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31972184).
All authors have read and agreed to the published version of the manuscript.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2020.105171.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
